title: Quantum circuit design via dynamic Pauli constraints
authors: James R. Wootton, Merlin Incerti-Medici, Daniel Bultrini, Pierre Fromholz
abstract: We introduce a novel software-oriented model of quantum computation motivated by the practical constraints of near-term quantum hardware. In this model, gates are specified by constraints expressed in terms of Pauli observables, with each disjoint layer of gates accompanied by a pairwise or $k$-local quantum state tomography of the device. We prove that the model is equivalent to the coupling-graph-restricted circuit model and hence universal for BQP, with a polynomial overhead: simulating a depth-$D$ circuit on $N$ qubits requires at most $O(D^2 N \log N)$ complexity. The model formalizes an idiom shared by existing work that ranges from quantum imaginary time evolution for the study of quantum systems to the use of quantum computers for procedural generation in games. It therefore provides a natural interface for designing quantum software entirely in terms of physically observable quantities, relevant for the NISQ era and into fault-tolerance.
summary: This paper proposes a new way to program quantum computers by specifying desired physical observables instead of complex gate sequences. It proves that this 'Motte model' is as powerful as the standard circuit model and provides a natural interface for NISQ-era algorithms like quantum imaginary time evolution.
main_problem: The need for a software-oriented quantum computing interface that uses physically observable quantities (Pauli expectation values) rather than complex unitary matrices, specifically addressing the constraints of the NISQ era.
main_result: The authors introduce and prove the universality of the 'Motte model,' a framework where gates are specified by Pauli constraints, showing it is equivalent to the standard circuit model with polynomial overhead.
method: The model uses k-body quantum state tomography to reconstruct reduced density matrices, which are then used to analytically deduce unitaries via eigenstate or density matrix transformations.
model_or_system: A software-oriented model of quantum computation (the Motte model) acting as an interface for a standard gate-model QPU with N qubits and a connected coupling graph G.
key_observables: Pauli expectation values (X, Y, Z) and k-local Pauli correlators.
important_parameters: N (number of qubits), D (circuit depth), k (tomographic locality), and delta (statistical sampling error).
main_assumptions: The underlying QPU is assumed to be noiseless for the theoretical definition, and statistical noise is assumed to be sufficient to lift eigenvalue degeneracies.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the Motte model workflow: user-provided Pauli targets lead to classical gate deduction, execution on a QPU, and iterative feedback via shot statistics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Motte model, establishes its computational universality (BQP equivalence), addresses the technical challenge of phase ambiguity in gate reconstruction, demonstrates practical applications like QITE and GHZ state preparation, and provides a complexity analysis.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Generic structure of the Motte model. The user works in the Pauli-expectation vocabulary; the classical in- terface deduces the gate; the QPU executes it. Repeated for D circuit-depth layers before a final Z-basis measurement.
